lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Layers exist that have no lyr:layer descriptions
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Every layer used in the spec has a lyr:layer element with at least an onboard mode

pr:section.pr:title: Layer Self-Description Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: For each layer namespace found in the spec's schemas, create a lyr:layer element describing it. Declare at minimum an onboard mode with prerequisites, conditions, and guidance. Register the layer description file in the index.

pr:ol.pr:li: Run clayers schema schemas/ to enumerate every layer namespace declared in the project
For each namespace, add a lyr:layer element with the matching schema attribute
Declare at least an onboard mode with lyr:condition and lyr:acceptance
Add other modes (catch-up, brainstorm, review) where they make sense for the layer
Register the layer descriptions file in index.xml

pr:section.pr:note: Mode bodies use xs:any with processContents="strict": every embedded element must validate against its own schema. Stick to known elements (pr:section, pr:p, etc.) and do not invent new ones.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: A new specification is being created
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: An index.xml exists listing all spec files with correct spec identity and version

pr:section.pr:title: Index Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Create the index.xml manifest file at the spec root. Declare the spec identity (spec:spec attribute) and version (spec:version). Add file elements for each spec file as they are created. The index defines the specification boundary and the ID-uniqueness domain.

pr:ol.pr:li: Create clayers/PROJECT/index.xml with a spec:clayers root and an idx:manifest element
Set spec:spec to the project identifier and spec:version to the schema version
Add an idx:file element for each XML file in the spec, including the index itself, with the primary layer attribute
Re-run validation after each file is added to confirm the manifest stays in sync

pr:section.pr:p: Validate immediately:
pr:section.pr:note: Files not registered in the index are invisible to validation, drift, and coverage. Always add the idx:file entry the same moment you create the file.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: A new specification is being created
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: A revision.xml exists with at least one named revision snapshot

pr:section.pr:title: Revision Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Create revision.xml with an initial named revision (typically draft-1). This snapshot anchors artifact mappings to a specific point in the spec's evolution, so node hashes are temporally meaningful.

pr:ol.pr:li: Create revision.xml in the spec directory
Add a rev:revision element with an id like rev-draft-1 and name="draft-1"
Set the timestamp to the creation time
Register the file in index.xml

pr:section.pr:note: Once mappings reference a revision name, do not rename it. Adding a new revision is fine; renaming an old one orphans every art:spec-ref that pointed to it.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: The project has version-controlled source code
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All repositories referenced by artifact mappings have vcs:git declarations with correct remote URLs

pr:section.pr:title: VCS Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Declare each git repository that will be referenced by art:artifact/@repo. Each repository becomes an addressable node, identified by its short ID (e.g., main for the primary repo).

pr:ol.pr:li: List the repositories that hold the implementing code for this spec
For each repo, add a vcs:git element with id, remote, and default-branch
Use the id attribute as the value of art:artifact/@repo in mappings

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Source code or documentation exists to describe
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Every major module and component has a pr:section with title, shortdesc, and descriptive paragraphs

pr:section.pr:title: Prose Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Create spec files organized by topic, NOT by layer type. One file per major module or component. Each file mixes prose, terminology, organization, relations, artifact mappings, and LLM descriptions for the same topic.

Every file contains pr:section elements with meaningful titles, short descriptions, and body paragraphs. Start with high-level architecture, then drill into subsystems. Write intent and design rationale, not code narration: the spec explains "why", source code already shows "what".

pr:ol.pr:li: Walk the source tree and identify major modules and components
For each module, create one spec file named after the topic (kebab-case)
Inside the file, declare the namespaces you actually use (see )
Add a top-level pr:section with title and pr:shortdesc
Use nested pr:section elements for subtopics (algorithms, data flow, error handling)
Register the file in index.xml with layer="urn:clayers:prose"
Write paragraphs that explain design rationale, not what the code does line by line

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template: and .
Validate after each file:
pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: organizing files by layer type (one file for all prose, one file for all artifact mappings). This makes maintenance painful because related elements are scattered. Always organize by topic.
Anti-pattern: shallow descriptions (a 2-sentence section for a 500-line module). The prose should match the conceptual depth of the code it describes.
Anti-pattern: code narration ("the function loops over the list and increments a counter"). Explain WHY the function exists, what problem it solves, what its callers expect.

lyr:mode.name: brainstorm
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: A new feature or component is being designed
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: New sections describe the feature's purpose, behavior, and design rationale

pr:section.pr:title: Prose Brainstorming
pr:section.pr:p: Write prose sections describing the new feature BEFORE code exists. Capture intent, constraints, expected behavior, and non-goals. These sections become the foundation for implementation and the contract that artifact mappings will later verify.

pr:ol.pr:li: Create a new topic file or open the existing file for the affected area
Add a pr:section describing the feature's purpose
Add nested sections for each subcomponent or behavior
State explicit non-goals: what the feature deliberately does NOT do
Cross-reference related concepts with pr:xref and trm:ref

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: catch-up
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Code modules exist without corresponding prose sections
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All code modules have prose descriptions

pr:section.pr:title: Prose Catch-Up
pr:section.pr:p: Identify code modules that lack prose coverage. Create sections for each, capturing what the module does and why it exists. Catch-up focuses on missing nodes; mapping quality is the artifact layer's review mode.

pr:ol.pr:li: Inventory existing prose sections: clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//pr:section/pr:title' --text
Compare against the directory structure under src/ (or your code root)
For each module without a section, read the code and write a section that captures its intent
Look for "shallow" sections (2-sentence prose for a 500-line module) and deepen them
After each batch, run validation and connectivity to catch isolated nodes

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Domain concepts exist in the codebase
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All domain concepts have trm:term definitions with precise, unambiguous definitions

pr:section.pr:title: Terminology Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Scan code identifiers, comments, and documentation for domain concepts. Each becomes a trm:term element with a canonical name and a precise definition that distinguishes it from similar concepts.

pr:ol.pr:li: Scan type, struct, class, and enum names for domain concepts
Scan function names, especially the public API surface
Scan constants and configuration keys
Extract concepts from comments and documentation
Group related terms into semantic clusters and place them in the topic file most relevant to each cluster
Write trm:term elements with definitions that distinguish each from similar concepts
Cross-reference related terms with trm:ref inside definitions
Add trm:ref elements in prose wherever the terms are used

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:
List existing terms before adding new ones to avoid duplicates:
pr:section.pr:note: Do not define trivial English words as terms. A term should be a domain-specific concept that has precise meaning in this project. "Database" is not a term; "Tenant Workspace" is.
IDs must be unique across the entire spec. Use the term- prefix and kebab-case.
Definitions must be self-contained. A reader who has never seen the codebase should understand the concept from the definition alone.

lyr:mode.name: brainstorm
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: New domain concepts are being introduced
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: New terms are defined before implementation begins

pr:section.pr:title: Terminology Brainstorming
pr:section.pr:p: When designing a new feature, identify new domain concepts that need controlled definitions. Define terms BEFORE writing code so that implementation uses consistent vocabulary from day one.

pr:ol.pr:li: List the new concepts the feature introduces
For each concept, draft a trm:term with a precise definition
Discuss the definition with stakeholders before locking it in (terms become part of the project's vocabulary)
Use the term names verbatim when naming types, functions, and variables in the eventual implementation

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: catch-up
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Code uses domain terms not yet defined in the spec
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: No undefined domain terms remain in the codebase

pr:section.pr:title: Terminology Catch-Up
pr:section.pr:p: Search for domain-specific identifiers and concepts in the code that lack trm:term definitions, then add the missing definitions.

pr:ol.pr:li: List existing terms: clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//trm:term/trm:name' --text
Compare against the codebase vocabulary (type names, function names, frequently-mentioned concepts in comments)
For every concept that recurs in the code without a term, add one
Add trm:ref in prose wherever the newly-defined terms appear

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Prose sections exist that need topic classification
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Every prose section has an org:concept, org:task, or org:reference classification with a purpose statement

pr:section.pr:title: Organization Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Classify each prose section by topic type. There are three types, and every classified element needs a org:purpose statement.

pr:ul.pr:li: org:concept: explanatory content; what something IS, how it works, why it was designed this way
org:task: system behavior or procedural content; what the system does when X happens
org:reference: lookup information; API endpoints, configuration keys, error codes

pr:ol.pr:li: For each pr:section, decide which type fits its content
Add the matching org:concept, org:task, or org:reference element with ref="section-id"
Always include a org:purpose statement explaining the classification
For tasks, add an org:actor when the subject is unclear

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:
pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: classifying everything as org:concept. If you find yourself reaching for concept by default, re-read the section and ask whether it describes a behavior (task) or a lookup table (reference).

lyr:mode.name: catch-up
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Prose sections exist without topic classification
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All sections are classified

pr:section.pr:title: Organization Catch-Up
pr:section.pr:p: Find prose sections missing topic typing and add the appropriate org:concept, org:task, or org:reference with a purpose statement.

pr:ol.pr:li: List all sections: clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//pr:section/@id' --text
List existing classifications: clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//org:concept/@ref | //org:task/@ref | //org:reference/@ref' --text
For each unclassified section, read it and add the appropriate typing element

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Multiple spec nodes exist that should be semantically linked
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: No isolated nodes remain in the connectivity graph (every node has at least one relation)

pr:section.pr:title: Relation Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Add typed relations between spec nodes to capture the semantic structure of the project. The target is zero isolated nodes: every node should have at least one relation in or out.
Relation types:

pr:ul.pr:li: depends-on: SOURCE requires TARGET to function (auth depends-on database)
refines: SOURCE is a more specific version of TARGET (jwt-auth refines auth)
references: SOURCE mentions or uses TARGET (api-handler references auth)
precedes: SOURCE must happen before TARGET (init precedes serve)
implements: SOURCE realizes the concept in TARGET (jwt-validator implements auth-policy)

pr:ol.pr:li: Run connectivity analysis to find isolated nodes: clayers connectivity clayers/PROJECT/
For each isolated node, identify the most natural relation to an existing node
Add a rel:relation with the right type, from, and to attributes
Always add a rel:note explaining why the relation exists
Re-run connectivity until no isolated nodes remain

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:
pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: circular depends-on chains. depends-on is acyclic by design; cycles indicate a structural mistake. Break the cycle by redirecting one of the relations or by introducing a third node that both depend on.
Anti-pattern: missing obvious dependencies. If the auth module uses the database module but no rel:relation records that, the connectivity graph is lying.

lyr:mode.name: catch-up
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Isolated nodes exist in the connectivity graph
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Zero isolated nodes in connectivity analysis

pr:section.pr:title: Relation Catch-Up
pr:section.pr:p: Run connectivity analysis. For each isolated node, identify its semantic relationships to other nodes and add the appropriate rel:relation elements.

pr:ol.pr:li: Run clayers connectivity clayers/PROJECT/ and read the "isolated nodes" section
For each isolated node, read the node content to understand what it represents
Find the closest related node and add a rel:relation
Re-run connectivity to confirm the node is no longer isolated

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: review
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Spec has been modified since last review
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Connectivity analysis shows no isolated nodes and relation types accurately reflect semantic meaning

pr:section.pr:title: Relation Review
pr:section.pr:p: Review the connectivity graph for accuracy. Verify relation types match the actual semantic relationships. Add missing relations. Remove or correct incorrect ones.

pr:ol.pr:li: Run clayers connectivity clayers/PROJECT/
Look for: isolated nodes, multiple connected components (suggests missing relations between clusters), acyclic violations (cycles in depends-on)
For each issue, decide whether to add a missing relation or fix an incorrect one
For new mappings or recently changed nodes, verify they are connected to the main component

pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: blindly adding references relations to fix isolated nodes. references is the weakest relation; if depends-on or refines actually fits, use that instead.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Architectural decisions have been made that should be recorded
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All significant architectural decisions have dec:decision records with status, alternatives, and rationale

pr:section.pr:title: Decision Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Identify architectural decisions in the codebase (framework choices, data model decisions, API design choices) and record them as dec:decision entries with status, rejected alternatives, and rationale.

pr:ol.pr:li: Scan the code, README, and design documents for decisions that shaped the project (e.g., "we use Postgres because...", "we chose REST over GraphQL because...")
For each decision, create a dec:decision referencing a pr:section that describes the decision in full
Set dec:status to accepted for decisions already in effect
For each rejected alternative, add a dec:alternative with rationale explaining why it was rejected

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: brainstorm
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: A design decision needs to be made
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: The decision is recorded with alternatives and rationale

pr:section.pr:title: Decision Brainstorming
pr:section.pr:p: When facing a design choice, create a dec:decision record in proposed status. Document alternatives with rationale before committing to a direction. Update status to accepted once decided, then to deprecated or superseded as the decision evolves.
Create ADRs for:

pr:ul.pr:li: Framework or library choices that affect the whole project
API design decisions (REST vs GraphQL, sync vs async)
Data model decisions (normalization, schema choices)
Cross-cutting infrastructure (auth strategy, observability stack)

pr:section.pr:p: Status lifecycle: proposed -> accepted -> deprecated -> superseded

pr:ol.pr:li: Write a pr:section describing the problem and constraints
Create a dec:decision referencing that section with status="proposed"
Enumerate alternatives as dec:alternative children, each with rationale
Once decided, update the status and document the accepted choice

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:
pr:section.pr:note: Document alternatives with the rationale for rejecting them, not just the chosen option. Future readers need to know what was considered and why it was set aside.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: External references (RFCs, papers, standards, documentation) are relevant to the project
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All referenced external materials have src:source declarations with proper classification

pr:section.pr:title: Source Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Identify external references mentioned in code comments, docs, or design discussions. Create src:source declarations with URLs, type classification, and optional cached content hashes.
Classify each external reference by type:

pr:ul.pr:li: paper: academic papers, research publications
rfc: IETF RFCs and similar standards-track documents
standard: ISO, W3C, or other formal standards
documentation: vendor docs, library guides

pr:ol.pr:li: Search code comments and docs for URLs, RFC numbers, paper citations
For each, add a src:source with id, type, title, and URL
For sources that may change, store a content hash so drift in the cited material is detectable
Link from prose using src:cite

lyr:mode.name: brainstorm
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: A feature design needs an implementation plan
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Implementation plan exists with items, acceptance criteria, and test references

pr:section.pr:title: Plan Brainstorming
pr:section.pr:p: Create a pln:plan with title, overview, status, and itemized steps. Each item has a description and acceptance criteria. Plans are versioned and queryable; they belong in the spec, not in side documents.
Plan structure:

pr:ul.pr:li: pln:title — short, action-oriented name
pln:overview — what this plan achieves and why
pln:status — proposed | active | completed
pln:item — one per step, each with its own acceptance criteria

pr:ol.pr:li: Write the plan against an existing prose section describing the feature
For each item, add at least one pln:criterion describing how to verify completion
Add witnesses to criteria when verification is executable: command for shell, script with lang attribute for executable scripts, manual with role for human verification
Use rel:relation type="depends-on" or type="precedes" to order items
Link items to spec nodes they implement (via prose embedding or relations)
Set status to proposed until work begins, active while in progress, then completed

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:
pr:section.pr:note: Embed prose elements (pr:p, pr:section, trm:ref, src:cite) directly inside plan items for rich descriptions. Plans are not limited to flat text.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Code files and spec nodes both exist
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Every spec node has an art:mapping with valid hashes and precise line ranges. Coverage analysis shows zero unmapped nodes.

pr:section.pr:title: Artifact Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Link each spec node to its implementing code. Create art:mapping elements with a spec-ref (node ID, revision, node-hash) and an artifact (repo, repo-revision, path, line ranges, content hash).

pr:ol.pr:li: Determine the current revision name from revision.xml (typically draft-1)
Determine the current repo commit: git rev-parse --short HEAD
For each spec node that describes code, create an art:mapping linking it to the implementing code
Use precise line ranges; multiple art:range elements per artifact are allowed for non-contiguous code
Write hashes as sha256:placeholder; the tooling will compute real values
For nodes with no implementing code (design rationale, methodology, abstract concepts), use art:exempt with a reason
Run hash fixers, then drift, then coverage

pr:section.pr:p: Use the templates: and
Line range precision targets:

pr:ul.pr:li: Precise (1-30 lines): PREFERRED. One function or one focused block.
Moderate (31-100 lines): acceptable for coherent multi-function modules.
Broad (101+ lines): AVOID. Split into multiple precise mappings.

pr:section.pr:p: Hash workflow:
pr:section.pr:codeblock: clayers artifact --fix-node-hash clayers/PROJECT/ clayers artifact --fix-artifact-hash clayers/PROJECT/ clayers artifact --drift clayers/PROJECT/ clayers artifact --coverage clayers/PROJECT/
pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: whole-file mapping. Mapping an entire 500-line file to one spec node loses precision and creates artifact drift on every unrelated change. Use function-level ranges.

Anti-pattern: self-referential mapping. Never map a spec XML file to itself as an artifact: editing the spec changes the artifact hash, creating infinite drift loops. Either exempt the node or map to non-spec code only.
Anti-pattern: duplicate IDs. Mapping IDs share the global ID namespace; collisions break the spec. Use the map- prefix and a descriptive name.

lyr:mode.name: catch-up
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Unmapped spec nodes or drifted mappings exist
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Zero unmapped nodes and zero drifted mappings

pr:section.pr:title: Artifact Catch-Up (Drift Resolution)
pr:section.pr:p: Run drift and coverage analysis. For drifted mappings, analyze WHY before fixing: blindly fixing hashes hides real changes. For unmapped nodes, create new art:mapping elements.
Drift output classifies each mapping:

pr:ul.pr:li: OK: hashes match on both sides; no action.
ARTIFACT DRIFTED: the code at the mapped range changed.
SPEC DRIFTED: the spec node prose changed.
UNAVAILABLE: the file or node was removed or renamed.

pr:section.pr:p: For ARTIFACT DRIFTED:

pr:ol.pr:li: Read the diff at the mapped range
Decide whether the change is formatting-only or semantic
Formatting only: update line ranges (if shifted) and re-fix the artifact hash
Semantic change: evaluate whether the spec node description still matches; update the prose if it no longer does
Run clayers artifact --fix-artifact-hash clayers/PROJECT/

pr:section.pr:p: For SPEC DRIFTED:

pr:ol.pr:li: Confirm the prose change is intentional
Run clayers artifact --fix-node-hash clayers/PROJECT/
Verify the mapped code still implements what the prose now describes

pr:section.pr:p: For UNAVAILABLE:

pr:ol.pr:li: Find the new path or ID, or remove the mapping
If the code was deleted, also reconsider whether the spec node should be removed

pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: blindly running --fix-artifact-hash on every drift without analyzing what changed. This masks real semantic divergence and defeats the purpose of drift detection.

lyr:mode.name: review
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Artifact mappings exist that may need quality improvement
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All mappings have precise line ranges, correct coverage levels, and descriptive notes

pr:section.pr:title: Artifact Review
pr:section.pr:p: Review artifact mappings for end-to-end quality. The goal is every spec node maps to code, every meaningful code region maps to a spec node, mappings are precise (small ranges, focused nodes), drift is zero, and exemptions are justified.

pr:section.pr:title: Spec-to-code coverage
pr:section.pr:p: For each unmapped spec node, decide whether to map or exempt:

pr:ol.pr:li: Run clayers artifact --coverage clayers/PROJECT/
For each unmapped node, read the node content with clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//*[@id="NODE_ID"]' --text
If it describes code, find the implementing code and create a precise mapping
If it is abstract (design rationale, methodology), add an art:exempt with reasoning

pr:section.pr:title: Code-to-spec coverage
pr:section.pr:p: For each uncovered code range, either extend an existing mapping or create a new spec node:

pr:ol.pr:li: Run clayers artifact --coverage clayers/PROJECT/ --code-path src/
For each NOT COVERED range, read the code and decide whether it is a distinct concept or part of an already-mapped one
If part of an existing concept, add another art:range to the existing mapping
If it is a new concept, create the spec node first (spec-first), then map it

pr:section.pr:title: Exemption audit
pr:section.pr:p: For each art:exempt, verify the reasoning is still valid:

pr:ol.pr:li: List exemptions: clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//art:exempt/@node' --text
For each, ask: did someone add implementing code since the exemption?
Could part of the node now be mapped even if the whole cannot?
If the exemption is no longer valid, remove it and add a mapping
Split Broad mappings (101+ lines) into multiple precise mappings
Verify art:coverage values (full vs partial) are accurate
Confirm every mapping has a descriptive art:note explaining what the code implements
If one node has 10+ mappings, it is too broad: split the node into focused sub-sections

pr:section.pr:title: Decision framework: map vs exempt
pr:section.pr:p: ALWAYS MAP: public API, core business logic, data types, algorithms, CLI commands, API endpoints, configuration schemas, constants, type definitions.

USUALLY EXEMPT: design decisions (dec:decision) that explain WHY, historical context that doesn't correspond to current code, methodology descriptions, future work items not yet implemented, generated code, trivial getters/setters, test fixtures.
ASK THE USER: internal utilities, error types, configuration, code in a different repository, emergent behavior with no single code location.
Default: map. Only exempt when you are confident there is truly no implementing code.
Useful commands:
pr:section.pr:codeblock: clayers artifact --coverage clayers/PROJECT/ clayers artifact --coverage clayers/PROJECT/ --code-path src/ clayers artifact --drift clayers/PROJECT/ clayers connectivity clayers/PROJECT/
pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: copy-paste line numbers without reading the code. Always read the code at the proposed range to confirm it implements what the spec node describes.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Spec nodes exist that need machine-readable descriptions
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Every spec node has an llm:node description. Every schema has an llm:schema description.

pr:section.pr:title: LLM Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Add llm:node descriptions for each spec node, explaining what it means, how it relates to other nodes, and guidance for interpreting or modifying it. Add llm:schema descriptions for each layer namespace.

pr:ol.pr:li: List existing nodes: clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//*[@id]/@id' --text
List existing LLM descriptions: clayers query clayers/PROJECT/ '//llm:node/@ref' --text
For each node without an LLM description, write one that starts with the most important information
Place the llm:node in the same file as the node it describes
For each layer namespace, add an llm:schema explaining the layer's purpose, main elements, and when to use it

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:
pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: generic descriptions that just restate the element name ("term-foo: this is the foo term"). Descriptions must add information that is not obvious from the ID or title.
Front-load important information. LLM descriptions are read top-to-bottom; the first sentence should answer "what is this and why does it matter?"

lyr:mode.name: catch-up
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Spec nodes exist without llm:node descriptions
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All nodes have descriptions

pr:section.pr:title: LLM Catch-Up
pr:section.pr:p: Find spec nodes missing llm:node descriptions and add them. Focus first on nodes that are most important for LLM comprehension: top-level sections, terms with non-obvious meaning, and nodes referenced by many relations.

pr:ol.pr:li: Compute the gap: list all node IDs and all llm:node/@ref values; the difference is uncovered
Prioritize sections, then terms, then everything else
For each, write a description that adds information beyond the title

pr:section.pr:p: Use the template:

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: External content needs to be referenced with integrity guarantees
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All external content references have cnt:content elements with valid hashes

pr:section.pr:title: Content Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: For external content that other layers reference, create cnt:content elements with content hashes for integrity verification. Content addressing makes the spec self-checking: the hash detects tampering or accidental modification.
Use cnt:content for:

pr:ul.pr:li: Test fixtures referenced by the testing layer
Cached external documents (papers, RFCs) where the source URL may change
XML templates that other parts of the spec link to
Data files referenced by examples

pr:ol.pr:li: For each piece of referenced content, decide whether to embed it (small, stable) or link to it (large, external)
For embedded content, place the body in a cnt:body element with a hash
For external content, declare the URL alongside the hash so the reference is verifiable
Reference the content from other layers via its ID

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Tests exist or need to be defined for spec verification
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: Key spec requirements have corresponding test definitions with appropriate types (manual, vectors, property, program, witness)

pr:section.pr:title: Testing Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Define test specifications for key requirements. Choose the test type based on what the test actually does: do not default to program when the verification is actually a manual checklist.
Test types:

pr:ul.pr:li: manual: human procedure that requires judgment
vectors: input/output pairs for deterministic functions
property: invariants that must hold for all inputs in some domain
program: executable test (unit, integration, end-to-end)
witness: external verification (third-party audit, formal proof artifact)

pr:ol.pr:li: For each requirement, identify what success looks like
Pick the test type that matches the verification style
Link the test to the spec node it verifies
For executable tests, link via artifact mapping to the test source code

pr:section.pr:note: Anti-pattern: classifying every test as program. A README walkthrough is manual; a table of expected outputs is vectors; a "for all valid inputs the output is sorted" is property.

lyr:mode.name: onboard
lyr:mode.lyr:condition: Python source code exists in the project
lyr:mode.lyr:acceptance: All public Python APIs have py:module, py:class, and py:function descriptions with parameter types and return types

pr:section.pr:title: Python API Onboarding
pr:section.pr:p: Describe the Python API surface. Create py:module elements for packages, py:class for classes, and py:function or py:method for callables. Include parameter lists with types, return types, and raised exceptions.

pr:ol.pr:li: Walk the Python package tree
For each module, add a py:module with the module's purpose
For each public class, add a py:class with attributes and methods
For each public function or method, add a py:function or py:method with parameters (name and type), return type, and raised exceptions
Skip private members (leading underscore) unless they are part of the documented API

pr:section.pr:note: The Python layer complements the artifact layer: artifact mappings link to specific code ranges, while Python descriptions capture the API contract that callers depend on.

rel:relation.rel:note: Terminology extraction requires prose to exist first, as terms are derived from and referenced within prose content.
Topic typing classifies prose sections, so prose must exist before organization can be applied.
Artifact mappings link spec nodes (primarily prose sections) to code, so spec nodes must exist first.
LLM descriptions reference spec nodes, which are primarily defined in the prose layer.
Artifact mappings reference VCS repositories, so repo declarations must exist first.
Artifact mappings pin to revisions, so at least one revision must exist.

spec:clayers.llm:node: The self-referential meta-layer: describes the layer layer itself. Its onboard mode tells orchestrators how to create lyr:layer instances for layers that lack descriptions.
The prose layer is the foundation that most other layers build on. Onboarding it first ensures spec nodes exist for terminology extraction, topic typing, relation building, and artifact mapping.
The artifact layer depends on the most other layers (prose, terminology, organization, VCS, revision) because it creates the bridge between spec and code. It is typically the last onboarding phase.
